scope.summary: A reusable wrapper that runs any heavy command under an enforced budget: isolated per-run target dir, MemoryMax, and TasksMax, emitting the resource_budget block the evidence contract requires.
out_of_scope: Changing /tmp/cargo-target: it is a bind mount onto ext4, already fixed by the operator under 56rf, and was not touched.
Making existing callers adopt the wrapper: version_matrix.sh, the live suite and CI still invoke cargo directly. This bead ships the harness; adopting it in each caller is not done.
Enforcing the budget in GitHub Actions: hosted runners have no systemd user session, so the wrapper refuses there rather than pretending. Local and self-hosted runs only.
The build-slot policy (56rf) and cargo jobs=4 throttle: both already applied, and neither is this wrapper's job.

entry_points.name: scripts/resource_budget.sh --profile build -- cargo check -p oracledb-derive
entry_points.trace: Real cargo run under the budget: completed in 2.54s with CARGO_TARGET_DIR propagated into the cgroup scope, cold isolated target dir, sccache-backed.

entry_points.name: scripts/check_resource_budget.sh
entry_points.trace: 7/7 checks. Kernel reports pids.max=256/memory.max=8G; 24 forks under TasksMax=16 give pids.peak=16 and pids.events max>0; tmpfs dir refused with exit 78; emitted budget satisfies the contract. 5 consecutive runs green.

entry_points.name: scripts/resource_budget.sh --profile mutants --emit-budget
entry_points.trace: Emits the resource_budget block consumed by required-proof/v1 and mutation-result/v1; validated field-by-field in check 5.

limitations: readiness.basis is scoped-test, so this close cannot claim ready: the Required graph (clippy, workspace test) was NOT run. The diff contains zero Rust and the shared checkout holds other panes' in-flight Rust, so a workspace run would report on their tree rather than this change.

source.tree_clean asserts that every file this close claims is committed at source.sha, verified with `git status --porcelain -- <scope.in_scope>`; it is not a claim that the shared working directory was pristine. See docs/BEAD_CLOSE_EVIDENCE.md.

Enforcement depends on systemd-run and a cgroup v2 user session. The wrapper refuses to run rather than running unbounded when systemd-run is absent, so on a host without it the harness gives no protection and says so.

The bounded-fanout proof exercises TasksMax at 16 with a moderate overshoot. It proves the kernel denies forks at the cap; it does not prove the specific 512/256 task numbers are the right budgets for real workloads, which are judgement calls that should be revisited if a legitimate run is refused.

MemoryMax is applied but not adversarially proven here: no test deliberately exceeds the memory cap, so the memory half rests on the kernel reporting memory.max correctly (check 1) rather than on an observed OOM-kill.
The harness is available but not yet wired into any caller, so nothing is protected by it until callers adopt it.
